1.synthesis: Moses’ psalm contrasts the eternity of God with the brevity of man: “Lord, you have been our dwelling place in all generations. Before the mountains were brought forth… from everlasting to everlasting you are God” (vv.1–2). Human life, by contrast, is swept away like a dream, like grass that flourishes in the morning and withers by evening (vv.5–6), our years “soon gone, and we fly away” (v.10). So the psalmist prays, “Teach us to number our days that we may get a heart of wisdom” (v.12). The integrative thread is the wisdom born of mortality before the eternal God: measuring our fleeting lives against God’s everlasting being humbles pride and presses us to live wisely in the few days we have.
1.historical_context: Attributed to Moses (title), the oldest psalm, this prayer reflects the wilderness generation’s experience of dying under God’s judgment (Numbers 14:29). The contrast of God’s eternity with human transience (vv.2–6) and the lifespan of “seventy… or eighty years” (v.10) draw on Israel’s mortality in the desert. The link between human death and God’s “anger” at sin (vv.7–9) frames mortality as the wage of sin.
1.literary_structure: Psalm 90 opens Book IV of the Psalter, its movement from God’s eternity (v.2) to human frailty (vv.5–6) to the prayer for wisdom (v.12) and restoration (vv.13–17). The imagery of grass and a watch in the night gives the meditation its elegiac tone, and the petition “teach us to number our days” is the hinge from lament to prayer.
1.theological_application: To “number our days” (v.12) — to live conscious of our mortality before the eternal God — is the path to a heart of wisdom (James 4:14). Reflecting on life’s brevity humbles pride, exposes the folly of living for this world, and presses us to seek what endures (Matthew 6:19–20). The believer finds his security not in his fleeting years but in the eternal God who is our “dwelling place in all generations” (v.1), the unchanging refuge across every age.
1.christology: The eternal God who is our dwelling place “from everlasting to everlasting” (v.2) is revealed in Christ, who is “before all things” (Colossians 1:17) and the same “yesterday and today and forever” (Hebrews 13:8). Into our grass-like frailty (v.5; 1 Peter 1:24) the eternal Son entered, sharing our mortality to redeem it, so that those who dwell in him pass from the brevity of this life into the eternal life he gives (John 11:25). He is the everlasting home of all generations of the faithful.

13.synthesis: The psalm turns to petition: “Return, O LORD! How long?… Satisfy us in the morning with your steadfast love, that we may rejoice and be glad all our days” (vv.13–14). Moses prays that God would give gladness proportionate to the days of affliction, show his work to his servants, and “establish the work of our hands” (vv.15–17). The integrative thread is the plea that the eternal God would lend lasting meaning to fleeting lives: having confronted human transience, the psalmist asks God to satisfy with steadfast love and to make the brief labors of mortal hands endure — that frail and dying people might yet do work of eternal worth through the favor of God.
13.historical_context: The prayer for satisfaction “in the morning” with steadfast love (chesed, v.14) seeks covenant mercy to answer the night of affliction. The plea to “establish the work of our hands” (v.17) asks that God give permanence to the labors of a people whose lives are otherwise as fleeting as grass — a poignant request from the wilderness generation whose works largely perished with them.
13.literary_structure: This closing petition (vv.13–17) answers the lament over transience with a prayer for God’s mercy and lasting favor. The movement from “how long?” (v.13) to “establish the work of our hands” (v.17) turns mortality’s despair toward hope grounded in God’s steadfast love, balancing the psalm’s somber opening with petition.
13.theological_application: Only God can give lasting worth to the labors of fleeting lives; we pray, “establish the work of our hands” (v.17), knowing that apart from him we labor in vain (Psalm 127:1). His steadfast love satisfies and gives joy that the brevity of life cannot steal (v.14). The believer is taught to seek God’s favor on his work and to do that which, done in the Lord, “is not in vain” (1 Corinthians 15:58), enduring beyond our few days.
13.christology: The satisfying “steadfast love” the psalmist seeks (v.14) is poured out in Christ, in whom the morning of God’s mercy has dawned (Luke 1:78). Through him the work of frail human hands is established with eternal worth, for what is done in the Lord abides (1 Corinthians 15:58). The fleeting life that longs to count for something finds its enduring meaning in union with the risen Christ, whose own finished work secures an unfading inheritance for his people (1 Peter 1:4). The God whose “steadfast love” satisfies in the morning (v.14) has visited his people in Christ, the dayspring from on high (Luke 1:78), turning the night of affliction into the morning of mercy. What frail hands build in him is established forever (1 Corinthians 15:58), so that the fleeting life finds eternal significance united to the risen Lord, whose own finished work secures an unfading inheritance (1 Peter 1:4).
